draft.introduction: Khwaja Shams ud-Din Hafiz-e Shirazi is the supreme lyric poet of the Persian language. His ghazals operate simultaneously as love poetry, wine songs, and Sufi mystical allegory, resisting any single interpretation.

translation.text: O cup-bearer, let the wine flow and pass it around, for love at first seemed easy, then the difficulties came. With the fragrance of musk that the morning breeze will unfold from those dark curls—what blood fell upon hearts from the twisting of each lock! In the assembly of the beloved, when the last intimacy is the call to depart, what will it matter if they say we are the people of the wine-house?
translation.original_text: الا یا ایها الساقی ادر کاسا و ناولها که عشق آسان نمود اول ولی افتاد مشکلها

translator_notes.note: Every key term operates on two levels: the saqi (cup-bearer) is both the tavern server and the divine dispenser of mystical intoxication; the wine is both grape wine and the ecstasy of divine love; the beloved's curls are both physical beauty and the entanglements of worldly attachment. Hafiz never resolves this ambiguity.
